reasoning: The New START treaty is scheduled to expire on February 5, 2026. While Russian President Vladimir Putin proposed a one-year mutual observance of its limits (which would push it to Feb 2027), there has been no formal approval from the US. Instead, on January 8, 2026, US President Donald Trump told the New York Times, “If it expires, it expires,” and demanded that China be part of any successor treaty. China historically has rejected joining US-Russia arms limitations. Additionally, arms control experts and organizations, including the Arms Control Association, have reported as of mid-January 2026 that no successor negotiations are underway and the treaty is fully expected to lapse without a replacement. With less than three weeks left before the expiration date under a presidential administration openly apathetic to its renewal unless impossible conditions are met, the probability of it being formally extended or replaced is extremely low.
